Bias Tracing Analysis Report

OLMo-2-0425-1B (Base + Instruct) · Pythia-1B · StereoSet domains: gender, profession, race, religion

Contents: 1. The Process 2. Score Definitions 3. How to Read the Graphs 4. Findings 5. Implications 6. Glossary

1. The Process — What We Are Measuring

We want to know: where inside a language model is bias encoded, and does that location change across training? The tool is causal tracing — it does not measure whether the model is biased in general; it localises which layer carries the bias signal.

The data — StereoSet sentence pairs

Each data point is a sentence pair:

  • Stereotyped: "The nurse said that she was very busy."
  • Anti-stereotyped: "The nurse said that he was very busy."

The subject is the word driving the stereotypic prediction — here, nurse. We measure whether the model assigns higher probability to the stereotyped completion.

DomainSentence pairsStatus
Gender~650Reliable
Race~930Reliable
Profession~520Reliable
Religion~30⚠ Unreliable — too few pairs

Three passes through the model

  1. Clean run — model reads the sentence normally. Record the probability on the stereotyped completion token. This is high_score.
  2. Corrupted run — add Gaussian noise to the subject token embeddings ("nurse"). The model can no longer read who the subject is. Record the new probability. This is low_score.
  3. Restoration — start from the corrupted run; at one specific (layer L, subject token T), replace the corrupted hidden state with the clean value from Pass 1. Measure how much the prediction recovers. Repeat for every layer.

Three restore conditions run in Pass 3:

  • States — full restore: both MLP and Attention outputs at layer L
  • Attn-only — only Attention output restored; MLP left corrupted
  • MLP-only — only MLP output restored; Attention left corrupted

This separates which computational pathway (MLP vs Attention) carries the bias at each layer.

2. Score Definitions

ScoreWhat it isTypical value (OLMo final)
high_scoreClean-run probability on the stereotyped completion. How confident the model is when it can see the subject normally.0.39–0.46
low_scoreCorrupted-run probability. Floor — model has lost the subject identity.0.30–0.34
effect_gaphigh − low. How much the model RELIES on knowing who the subject is. <0.03 = unreliable signal.0.07–0.12
states_nie[L]Mean indirect effect (States restore) at layer L, averaged over all subject-token positions and all sentence pairs. This is the Y-axis of every bar and line chart.0.27–0.37
attn_nie[L]Same for Attn-only restore.Low, ~0.27–0.34
mlp_nie[L]Same for MLP-only restore.Low, ~0.27–0.34
Important: states_nie, attn_nie, mlp_nie are raw restoration probabilities — the same unit as the Y-axis on all charts. They are not normalised by the effect gap unless explicitly stated. A higher value means restoring that layer recovers more of the biased prediction.

What does "indirect effect declining across layers" mean?

The score at layer L is highest at L0 and declines toward L15. This is not the model becoming less biased at deeper layers. Bias is present throughout. The score declines because restoring a single hidden state at a later layer creates an inconsistent internal state — that layer's clean state is surrounded by corrupted context from all other layers and tokens. The inconsistency limits how much recovery is possible.

Restoring L0 is the most upstream intervention: the correct embedding propagates forward through all 15 subsequent layers, giving it maximum leverage. Restoring L14 is nearly too late — the prediction is almost made, and the restored state is inconsistent with everything before it.

3. How to Read the Graphs

Graph type 1 — Standard bar chart (one checkpoint, one domain)

  • X-axis: Transformer layer. Layer 0 = the token embedding; Layer 15 = final transformer layer before the output head.
  • Y-axis: |log_prob(stereo) − log_prob(anti)| after restoring layer L. Higher = model more strongly prefers the stereotyped completion. Same unit as the original bar charts.
  • Blue bars (States): Full restore — the total causal effect at this layer.
  • Red bars (Attn-only): How much the Attention pathway alone contributes.
  • Green bars (MLP-only): How much the MLP pathway alone contributes.
Pattern to look for: Blue bars tallest at L0, declining toward L15. Red and green bars consistently low and flat — neither Attention nor MLP alone carries much signal.

Graph type 2 — Line checkpoint plot (all checkpoints, one domain)

  • X-axis: Layer (0–15). Same as the bar chart.
  • Y-axis: Abs. log prob diff (stereo − anti). Same unit as the bar chart — directly comparable.
  • Each line: One training checkpoint. Light = early training, dark = late training.
  • Dashed line: That checkpoint has effect gap < 0.03 — estimates unreliable.
  • Three panels: States / Attn-only / MLP-only — same as the three bar colours.
  • Y-axis is shared across all three panels — you can directly compare magnitudes.
Pattern to look for: Lines shift upward as training progresses (more bias acquired) but do NOT change shape. The slope from L0 to L15 is established early and never rotates. Attn-only and MLP-only panels show flat, low lines at all checkpoints.

Graph type 3 — Base vs Instruct line plot

  • Blue solid lines: Base pre-training checkpoints (0B → s2-51B).
  • Orange dashed lines: Instruct fine-tuning checkpoints (step200–step2600).
  • Y-axis shared between base and instruct — positions of dashed vs solid lines are directly comparable.
  • Circle marker: L0 position on base lines. Square marker: L0 on instruct lines.
Pattern to look for: Instruct dashed lines sit above the final base solid line (bias strengthened). Slopes are identical (same layer structure). The gap between instruct lines is small (barely changed during instruction tuning).

Graph type 4 — Effect gap bar chart

  • X-axis: Training checkpoints in order — base first, then instruct.
  • Y-axis: Effect gap = high_score − low_score. How much the model relies on knowing who the subject is.
  • Dashed vertical line: Separates base pre-training from instruct fine-tuning.
Pattern to look for: Gap is near-zero at 0B, grows through pre-training, then jumps at the first instruct checkpoint and stays flat for the rest of instruction tuning.

4. Findings

Finding 1

Bias is lexically encoded at the embedding layer (L0)

For both OLMo and Pythia, at every trained checkpoint, the highest indirect effect is at Layer 0 — the token embedding — and declines monotonically toward the final layer. Neither MLP-only nor Attn-only scores show any peak at any layer.

DomainL0L4L8L12L15
Gender0.3390.3280.3100.3010.301
Profession0.3240.3110.2920.2770.274
Race0.3270.3200.3010.2930.293

OLMo base, final checkpoint (s2-51B), States restore.

Why L0? Layer 0 is the token embedding — the vector that represents the word "nurse" or "father" before any transformer computation. This vector was learned from co-occurrence statistics in training data: "nurse" appeared near "she" far more than near "he". The stereotypic signal is encoded in the word's vector directly.

Restoring L0 gives the model back the correct word identity from the very start, allowing it to propagate through all 15 subsequent layers. This is why it has the highest causal leverage.

Contrast with factual recall (ROME paper): for facts like "Eiffel Tower → Paris", the peak causal effect is at a specific mid-layer MLP. The transformer actively computes a structured lookup there. For bias, no such peak exists — the transformer propagates the stereotype without amplifying or concentrating it.
Finding 2

Bias is acquired early in training, then consolidated

Gender domain, OLMo base, States restore at L0:

CheckpointTokensL0 scoreEffect gap
0B00.1050.057 (noisy)
21B21 billion0.3180.029
315B315 billion0.3140.050
2.4T2.4 trillion0.3200.068
4T4 trillion0.3180.059
s2-3Bstage 2 +3B0.3350.076
s2-51Bstage 2 +51B0.3390.075

Two distinct phases:

  1. 0B → 21B: The raw L0 score jumps from 0.105 to 0.318 — a massive early leap. The model learned the linguistic statistics of stereotypic words very fast. But the effect gap shrinks (0.057 → 0.029) because the model does not yet strongly depend on the subject's identity for its biased predictions.
  2. 21B → 4T: The raw L0 score barely moves. The effect gap grows steadily (0.029 → 0.068) — the model increasingly relies on knowing who the subject is.
  3. Stage 2 (s2-3B → s2-51B): Both metrics stabilise. Stage 2 training did not introduce new bias.
The L0-dominant declining shape is established at 21B and never changes. In the line plot, all checkpoint lines are parallel — they shift up in level but do not rotate or develop new peaks at any layer.
Finding 3

Instruction tuning increases bias strength but does not change its location

Profession domain (most dramatic change):

CheckpointL0L8L15Effect gap
Base s2-51B0.3240.2920.2740.097
Instruct step2000.3690.3230.3050.119 (+23%)
Instruct step14000.3710.3240.3050.122 (+26%)
Instruct step26000.3710.3240.3060.120 (+24%)

Three observations:

  1. Same layer structure. The slope from L0 to L15 is identical in base and instruct. L0 is still the peak. Instruction tuning did not move bias to different layers.
  2. Effect gap increased by ~24% for profession and race. The instruct model relies more on the subject's identity for its biased predictions. Instruction tuning strengthened the bias signal rather than reducing it.
  3. The change happened at step200 and then froze. From step200 to step2600 the gap barely moves. The model recalibrated at the very start of instruction tuning and stopped changing.
Gender shows a smaller change (~4% gap increase). Profession and race show ~24%. Religion is unreliable (too few cases).
Finding 4

The pattern is consistent across architectures

OLMo-2 uses a SwiGLU FFN with rotary position embeddings. Pythia uses a standard GPT-NeoX architecture. Both show the same L0-dominant, monotonically-declining indirect effect with flat MLP-only and Attn-only panels.

Notable difference in Pythia race: At late checkpoints (step81k–143k), the L0→L15 slope is steeper than OLMo's equivalent, and the effect gap is larger (0.112 vs 0.094). This could reflect training data differences or architecture-specific embedding geometry — it warrants further investigation.

Core finding is architecture-independent: L0 dominance, no MLP/Attn pathway peak. The specific magnitude and slope may vary.
Finding 5

MLP and Attention pathways carry negligible independent bias signal

In the bar chart and line plots, the red (Attn-only) and green (MLP-only) values are consistently low and flat across all layers, all checkpoints, and both models.

For factual recall, the ROME paper identifies specific MLP layers where the indirect effect peaks sharply — those layers act as key-value stores for the fact. For bias, no such MLP peak exists. Neither the MLP nor the Attention pathway alone has a high-indirect-effect layer.

This is the mechanistic reason why ROME/MEMIT-style layer-targeted editing is unlikely to work for debiasing — there is no locatable MLP storage site to edit.

5. Implications for Debiasing

Layer-targeted editing methods (ROME, MEMIT, MEND)

These methods find the causally important MLP layer via causal tracing, then modify weights there. They work for facts because facts have a locatable MLP peak. For bias, causal tracing finds no such peak — L0 dominance means the signal enters through the embedding, not any MLP computation. Editing mid-layer MLPs for debiasing is targeting the wrong location.

Embedding-space approaches

Methods that operate on the token embedding matrix directly — Hard Debias, INLP, embedding fine-tuning — are mechanistically better aligned with where the bias lives. The causal tracing results provide a mechanistic justification for why embedding-level interventions are the right target.

Instruction tuning

Instruction tuning (RLHF/SFT) increased the effect gap for profession and race by ~24% rather than reducing it. It did not change the causal location of bias. Post-training alignment does not appear to address lexically-encoded stereotypic associations and may amplify them.

6. Glossary

TermDefinition
Subject tokensThe words in the sentence whose identity drives the stereotypic prediction (e.g., "nurse", "father")
CorruptionAdding Gaussian noise to subject token embeddings — breaks the model's ability to read who the subject is
RestorationReplacing a corrupted hidden state at one (layer, token) position with its clean value from the uncorrupted run
Indirect effectThe restoration score at a given layer — how much the biased prediction recovers when only that layer is restored
Effect gaphigh_score − low_score — how much the model relies on the subject's identity. <0.03 = unreliable
L0Layer 0, the embedding layer — the raw token vector before any transformer computation
States restoreFull restoration at a layer — both MLP and Attention hidden states replaced with clean values
MLP-only restoreOnly the MLP output restored; Attention output left corrupted
Attn-only restoreOnly the Attention output restored; MLP output left corrupted
Low-signalEffect gap < 0.03 — corruption barely changes the prediction; indirect effects are unreliable (shown as dashed lines)
